title: Google I/O 2025 – U8Nb68XsVY4
text:
MIGUEL RAMOS: Hello. I'm Miguel Ramos. I'm a Senior Product
Manager in Firebase. And I am with my colleague,
Thomas Ezan, a Senior Android Developer Relations Engineer. Today, we will explore how
to enhance your Android app using Google's generative
models via Firebase. We will start by talking about
how to use the Gemini Pro and Flash series models
in your Android app, and the steps to follow
to use them in production. Then we'll demonstrate the Live
API, a new Gemini capability for bidirectional streaming, and
how you can experiment with it today. Finally, we'll talk about
Imagen 3, Google's latest image generation model. Let's kick things off. First, let's take a look at
the Gemini Flash and Pro models that are run in the Cloud
and how you can use them in your Android app. Gemini models can
process and analyze many types of information, like
text, images, audio, and video. The specific capability
depends on which Gemini model you use, like Flash, Flash Lite,
Pro, or different versions, like 2.0 or 2.5. A key feature is the large
context window, basically, how much the model
can process at once. For example, the
Gemini 2.0 Flash can handle up to 1 million
tokens in a single request. It allows you to send very
large amounts of information, like one hour of video,
or about 750,000 words. Generally, when you give
the model more context, it gives you more
consistent answers. A large context
window also makes it possible to create
much longer experiences. To get access and use the
Gemini functionalities in your application, you will
need to leverage the Gemini API. Currently, Google
offers two Gemini API with similar features, one in
Google AI Studio, also known as Gemini Developer API,
or just the Gemini API, and another in Vertex AI,
the Gemini API in Vertex AI. Back in October,
Firebase [INAUDIBLE] a [? set ?] client
SDK that gives you access to the Gemini API
in Vertex AI directly from your client code without
having to touch the backend. Currently, developer are
confidentially using it
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – U8Nb68XsVY4
text:
Back in October,
Firebase [INAUDIBLE] a [? set ?] client
SDK that gives you access to the Gemini API
in Vertex AI directly from your client code without
having to touch the backend. Currently, developer are
confidentially using it in the production apps. At Google I/O, the Firebase SDK
supports the Gemini Developer API as well, providing a single
SDK for accessing any Gemini API from client apps. The Gemini Developer API offers
a generous no-cost tier with a flexible pay-as-you-go
plan to help you scale. So you don't have
any more excuses to start adding generative AI to
your apps with Firebase anymore. Let's see how easy it is to
add Gemini to your Android app in Kotlin. This is real code. And as you can see, it only
requires three lines of code to add generative
AI to your app. Yep, only three. First line? Instantiate the model using
the Gemini Developer API. Second line? Generate content with a prompt. Third line? Get the response and process it. And that's it. As I promise, the experience
is pretty straightforward. THOMAS EZAN: Many
developers are already using AI to augment their apps
with exciting, intelligent features. For example, the
app Meal Planner uses Gemini 2.0 Flash
to generate meal plans. First, the user selects their
diet and the number of people, and Gemini generates a
meal plan for the week, with cooking instructions
and ingredients. And the meals of the previous
week are added to the context to avoid repetition when
generating a new meal plan. The Voice Recorder app from the
developer Smart Mobile Tools has been using Gemini to
summarize the transcription of voice notes. The transcription
of the voice note is sent to Gemini to
identify key points and deliver
actionable summaries. It saves the users time and
increases their productivity. And finally, the sleep
tracking app by Urbandroid is using Gemini 2.0
Flash to provide users with a one-sentence
summary of their sleep. It identifies trends in their
sleep patterns from last week, compared to their sleep history.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – U8Nb68XsVY4
text:
And finally, the sleep
tracking app by Urbandroid is using Gemini 2.0
Flash to provide users with a one-sentence
summary of their sleep. It identifies trends in their
sleep patterns from last week, compared to their sleep history. It also highlights both
strengths and weaknesses in their sleep patterns, with
suggestions for improvements. So as you can see,
you can already use the Gemini models in your
production app via Firebase. Now, let's talk about
good practices to follow. First, implement App Check. You can think of it as a
security guard for your backend. It helps prevent abuse by
identifying and blocking suspicious traffic, ensuring
that only authorized requests get through. It uses Play
Integrity to guarantee that all the requests come
from your authentic app on a genuine untampered device. After setting it
up, you can easily monitor your app's requests
in the Firebase Console and keep a close eye on
the traffic it generates. And Firebase App Check
isn't just for Android. It also supports
iOS and web apps, giving you a comprehensive
protection across the board. And you can even
extend this protection to your own backend
resources, adding an extra layer of security. Then use Firebase Remote Config. It allows you to
dynamically manage server-controlled
variables, such as the name of the model your app uses. This allows you to adapt
to the frequent changes in the generative AI landscape
without requiring app updates. When working with multimodal
prompts that include images, audio, or video, especially
for larger files, directly sending the data
from the user's device can be inefficient and prone to
issues on unreliable networks. Cloud storage for Firebase
offers a much better approach. You can efficiently upload
files to Cloud Storage, and then simply use the storage
URL or js link in your prompt. In addition, Firebase
security rules provide granular control
over the file access, ensuring that only the
authorized users can interact with the uploaded content. Finally, let's talk
about cost estimations.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – U8Nb68XsVY4
text:
In addition, Firebase
security rules provide granular control
over the file access, ensuring that only the
authorized users can interact with the uploaded content. Finally, let's talk
about cost estimations. The input and output size
of generative AI models is measured in tokens. A lot of words are
represented by a single token, but some more complicated
or less frequent words are split into two,
three, or more tokens. In our example, the
word "generative" is split into two
tokens, for instance. A good rule of thumb is
that 70 English words translate to about 100 tokens. But precisely evaluating the
number of tokens in your request is important because
each model has a limit on the number of
input tokens it can handle. We call it the context window. And the cost of the
query is directly based on the number of tokens
processed by the model. To easily compute
the number of tokens, you can use the countTokens
function in your Kotlin code. And you can read more about
the maximum size of the context window and the cost of inference
of each model in the Firebase documentation. MIGUEL RAMOS: It's time to
dive into the exciting world of the Gemini Live capabilities. The Live API allows you to build
real-time experiences using the bidirectional streaming
capabilities of Gemini. You need to handle the
WebSockets communication channels and create a
client server solution to support this in your app. Fortunately, the
Firebase SDK allows you to use the Live API
without needing to deal with all these complexities. We really did all
this work for you. Using the public preview
functionalities of the Firebase Android SDKs, you
can continuously send a string of text, audio, or
even video to the Gemini model and receive a real-time
streaming audio or text response. Imagine the
possibility for getting truly interactive and natural
AI experiences in your apps. Now let's do a live demo. These Android apps use
the Gemini Live API to let you talk to the
model about colors. Pressing Start begins
a two-way audio stream
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – U8Nb68XsVY4
text:
truly interactive and natural
AI experiences in your apps. Now let's do a live demo. These Android apps use
the Gemini Live API to let you talk to the
model about colors. Pressing Start begins
a two-way audio stream between you and the model. The model can change
the background color based on your voice input,
using a function call to identify the requested color
with the Gemini 2.0 Flash Live. Hello, there. SPEAKER: Hi, I'm your AI color
assistant, ready to help. What color would
you like to see? MIGUEL RAMOS: I want
to see the red color. SPEAKER: All right. Changing it to red now. MIGUEL RAMOS: Can
you make it lighter? SPEAKER: OK, I'll lighten it up. MIGUEL RAMOS: And what's
the color of happiness? SPEAKER: OK, I'm setting the
color to a bright yellow, like sunshine, to
represent happiness. MIGUEL RAMOS: And what
the color of sadness? SPEAKER: Got it. I'm changing the color
to a deep blue-gray, reflecting a somber mood. MIGUEL RAMOS: Thanks. You were pretty helpful. As you saw, the model was able
to fulfill complex requests, such as identifying the color of
moods and explain his reasoning. Pretty cool, huh? But do you know what
else is pretty cool? How easy it was to
create this experience. Four code instructions
are all you need. First, initialize the service
and create a live model instance with your configuration. Here we are using
Gemini Flash Live, requesting audio [INAUDIBLE]
as the modalities, setting some instruction
from the model and defining the
function calling tool. Second, we open up persistent
connection to the Gemini API. Third, we start the
audio conversation when the user presses
the Start button. And fourth, we stop
the conversation when the user presses
the Stop button. And that's it. All this magic in fewer steps
then letter in the "magic" word. THOMAS EZAN: We just covered
the Gemini Pro and Flash series models. Let's now switch gears to
talk about image generation. Later last year, Google
launched Imagen 3, our IS text-to-image model,
capable of generating images with even better detail.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – U8Nb68XsVY4
text:
THOMAS EZAN: We just covered
the Gemini Pro and Flash series models. Let's now switch gears to
talk about image generation. Later last year, Google
launched Imagen 3, our IS text-to-image model,
capable of generating images with even better detail. It is designed to generate
well-composed images with exceptional details,
reduced artifacts, and rich lighting. It represents a
substantial advancement in image generation technology. In February, we made Imagen
accessible through Firebase, simplifying the process to
integrate its capabilities to your Android app. It unlocks new possibilities
for Android developers. Generated visuals
can be tailored to the content of your app and
create a richer user experience. For example, users can
generate custom artwork in their in-app
profiles, or you can use Imagen to elevate
your app's storytelling and integrate contextualized
illustrations as part of an existing screen flow. And if your app
is using stickers, you can let your users
create personal and custom virtual stickers that only
they will be able to use. The integration of Imagen
3 to your Android app is very similar to adding Gemini
access via the Firebase AI SDK. Start by adding
Gradle dependencies to your Android project. In your Kotlin code, create
an image model instance by passing the model name. As an option, you can also
add the Model Configuration and safety settings. And finally, generate
the image by simply calling generateImages. You can then retrieve
the image generating as a bitmap from
the image response and display it in your app. The two available
imaging models are Imagen 3.0, which produces
realistic, high-quality images from natural language
text prompts, and Imagen 3.0 Fast, which
generates images for prototyping or low-latency use cases. You can experiment with image
prompts in Vertex AI Studio and find ways to refine your
prompts by consulting the imagen documentation. Imagen 3 in Firebase is
currently in public preview, so you can test it today.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – U8Nb68XsVY4
text:
You can experiment with image
prompts in Vertex AI Studio and find ways to refine your
prompts by consulting the imagen documentation. Imagen 3 in Firebase is
currently in public preview, so you can test it today. It is expected to be generally
available later this year. We covered a lot
of ground today. Let's take a minute to recap. The Firebase AI SDK is
production-ready today. You can add generative AI
capabilities to your application quickly and seamlessly. When you are ready
to go to production, use App Check to safeguard your
backend resources from abuse. Use Remote Config to remotely
update the model version. Take advantage of Cloud
Storage to upload big files and support unreliable networks. And the card token
function will enable you to evaluate your
costs before going live. We demonstrated
the Gemini Live API and how you can use it to create
conversational AI experiences, and it is ready for
prototyping today. And finally, Imagen 3 through
Firebase is in public preview. It enables you to add custom
contextual visual assets to increase your
app's engagement. If you want to learn more
about these generative AI SDKs, you will find all the details
in the Firebase documentation. Also, to learn more about
AI and Android in general, go to the AI Hub in the
Android public documentation. And finally, we published a
new Android sample app, which gathers a list of use cases. It lets you interact
with AI-powered features that you can easily integrate
to your application. During this talk,
we focused on how to integrate models running in
the Cloud into your Android app. But if you are interested in
learning about Gemini Nano, you can check out the talk,
Gemini Nano, on Android. Firebase is really
unlocking new capabilities to make your app more
engaging, and we can't wait to see what you'll build. Thank you for watching. [MUSIC PLAYING]
speaker: unknown
